content:
# Joshua Chapter 24 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 248-249
**Source Image:** `JOS_024_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_024_page_002.jpg`

1. And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and he summoned the elders of Israel, and their heads, and their judges, and their officers; and they stationed themselves before God.

2. And Joshua said to all the people: "Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: 'Your fathers dwelt beyond the River from of old — Terah, the father of Abraham and the father of Nahor — and they served other gods.

3. And I took your father Abraham from beyond the River, and I led him through all the land of Canaan, and I multiplied his seed, and I gave him Isaac.

4. And I gave to Isaac, Jacob and Esau. And I gave to Esau Mount Seir to possess it, but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.

5. And I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt according to what I did in its midst, and afterward I brought you out.

6. And I brought your fathers out of Egypt, and you came to the sea. And the Egyptians pursued your fathers with chariots and with horsemen to the Sea of Reeds.

7. And they cried out to YHWH, and he set darkness between you and the Egyptians, and he brought the sea upon them and covered them. And your eyes saw what I did in Egypt. And you dwelt in the wilderness many days.

8. And I brought you into the land of the Amorites who dwelt on the other side of the Jordan, and they fought with you. And I gave them into your hand, and you possessed their land, and I destroyed them from before you.

9. Then Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, arose and fought against Israel. And he sent and summoned Balaam son of Beor to curse you.

10. But I was not willing to listen to Balaam, and he surely blessed you, and I delivered you from his hand.

11. And you crossed over the Jordan and came to Jericho. And the lords of Jericho fought against you — the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Girgashites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — and I gave them into your hand.

12. And I sent the hornet before you, and it drove them out from before you — the two kings of the Amorites — not by your sword and not by your bow.

13. And I gave you a land for which you did not labor, and cities that you did not build, and you dwell in them. Vineyards and olive groves that you did not plant — you eat from them.'

14. Now therefore, fear YHWH and serve him in wholeness and in faithfulness, and put away the gods that your fathers served beyond the River and in Egypt, and serve YHWH.

15. And if it is evil in your eyes to serve YHWH, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve — whether the gods that your fathers served which were beyond the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are dwelling. But as for me and my house, we will serve YHWH."

16. And the people answered and said: "Far be it from us to forsake YHWH, to serve other gods!

17. For YHWH our God — he is the one who brought us and our fathers up from the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage, and who did these great signs before our eyes, and preserved us in all the way that we went, and among all the peoples through whose midst we passed.

18. And YHWH drove out all the peoples, and the Amorites dwelling in the land, from before us. We also will serve YHWH, for he is our God."

19. And Joshua said to the people: "You are not able to serve YHWH, for he is a holy God. He is a jealous God. He will not forgive your transgressions and your sins.

20. If you forsake YHWH and serve foreign gods, then he will turn and do you harm and consume you, after he has done good to you."

21. And the people said to Joshua: "No, but YHWH we will serve."

22. And Joshua said to the people: "You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen for yourselves YHWH, to serve him." And they said: "We are witnesses."

23. "Now therefore, put away the foreign gods that are in your midst, and incline your heart to YHWH, the God of Israel."

24. And the people said to Joshua: "YHWH our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey."

25. And Joshua made a covenant with the people on that day, and he set for them a statute and an ordinance in Shechem.

26. And Joshua wrote these words in the scroll of the Torah of God. And he took a great stone and set it up there, under the oak that was in the sanctuary of YHWH.

27. And Joshua said to all the people: "Behold, this stone shall be a witness against us, for it has heard all the words of YHWH that he spoke with us. And it shall be a witness against you, lest you deny your God."

28. And Joshua sent the people away, each man to his inheritance.

29. And it was after these things that Joshua son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died, being one hundred and ten years old.

30. And they buried him in the territory of his inheritance, in Timnath-serah, which is in the hill country of Ephraim, on the north of Mount Gaash.

31. And Israel served YHWH all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders who prolonged their days after Joshua, and who had known all the work of YHWH that he had done for Israel.

32. And the bones of Joseph, which the Sons of Israel had brought up out of Egypt, they buried in Shechem, in the portion of the field that Jacob had acquired from the sons of Hamor, the father of Shechem, for one hundred qesitah; and they became an inheritance for the sons of Joseph.

33. And Eleazar son of Aaron died, and they buried him in the Hill of Phinehas his son, which had been given to him in the hill country of Ephraim.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Joshua 24 is the final chapter of the book — the theological climax of the conquest narrative, recording the covenant assembly at Shechem, YHWH's first-person historical recital through Joshua, the great covenant challenge and the people's threefold commitment, followed by three burial notices (Joshua, Joseph's bones, and Eleazar) that close the book. The chapter divides into three major sections: YHWH's speech recounting Israel's history (vv. 1-13), the covenant dialogue between Joshua and the people (vv. 14-28), and the burial epilogue (vv. 29-33).

The three translators produced notably consonant renderings of this chapter. The structural backbone is identical across all three: the assembly frame (v. 1), YHWH's historical recital in the first person (vv. 2-13), Joshua's covenant challenge (vv. 14-15), the threefold exchange between Joshua and the people (vv. 16-24), the covenant formalization (vv. 25-27), the dismissal (v. 28), and the three burials (vv. 29-33). All three correctly preserved: YHWH as the divine name throughout all eighteen occurrences; the keyword repetition of "serve" (avad) which structures the entire chapter's theology; the infinitive absolute construction in v. 10; the personification of the witness-stone in v. 27; and the triple burial conclusion.

The Leningrad Codex manuscript (folios 248-249) was examined to verify readings. The text is clearly legible across both pages, written in the standard three-column format with Masoretic notation. The chapter begins in the continuation from Joshua 23 and runs through to the end of the book of Joshua, with the closing Masoretic colophon visible at the end of the text on folio 249. No significant scribal corrections or erasures were observed in the photographed pages.

Where all three translators agreed, the consensus follows their shared reading without alteration. Where they diverged, SOPHIA weighed manuscript evidence, Hebrew fidelity, and English clarity to select or synthesize the best reading, as documented in the disagreement log below.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 — Proper nouns (global)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Yehoshua," "Yisrael," "Kena'an," "Mitsrayim," etc. — full Hebrew transliteration |
| LOGOS | "Joshua," "Israel," "Canaan," "Egypt," etc. — anglicized |
| PNEUMA | "Joshua," "Israel," "Canaan," "Egypt," etc. — anglicized |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts anglicized forms throughout, consistent with the established convention from Joshua 1. Two-to-one agreement. This applies globally: Joshua (not Yehoshua), Israel (not Yisrael), Shechem (not Sh'khem), Terah (not Terach), Abraham (not Avraham), Nahor (not Nachor), Canaan (not Kena'an), Isaac (not Yitschak), Jacob (not Yaakov), Esau (not Esav), Seir (not Se'ir), Egypt (not Mitsrayim), Jordan (not Yarden), Zippor (not Tsippor), Balaam (not Bil'am), Beor (not Be'or), Jericho (not Yericho), Hittites (not Chittites), Hivites (not Chivvites), Jebusites (not Yevusites), Ephraim (not Efrayim), Joseph (not Yosef), Hamor (not Chamor), Eleazar (not El'azar), Phinehas (not Pinechas).

---

### Verse 1 — "summoned" vs. "called for"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he called for the elders of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "he summoned the elders of Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "He summoned the elders" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "summoned" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew vayyiqra le- in the context of a formal assembly conveys official summoning, not casual calling. "Summoned" captures the gravity of the covenantal convocation. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 1 — "stationed themselves" vs. "presented themselves"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "they stationed themselves before God" |
| LOGOS | "they presented themselves before God" |
| PNEUMA | "they presented themselves before God" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "stationed themselves" (EMET). Though "presented themselves" has two-to-one support, the Hebrew Hithpael vayyityatzvu (from yatzav) specifically means to take one's stand, to station oneself — a deliberate, formal positioning. "Presented themselves" is softer and loses the physical, military connotation of taking a covenantal stance. EMET's rendering preserves the Hebrew verb's force. However, the two-to-one weight is noted. SOPHIA ultimately retains EMET's reading because the Hithpael stem of yatzav carries a distinct semantic range from "present," and this is a covenantal assembly where formal positioning matters.

---

### Verse 2 — "beyond the River" vs. "on the other side of the River" vs. "beyond the Euphrates"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "On the other side of the River" |
| LOGOS | "beyond the River" |
| PNEUMA | "beyond the Euphrates" (first occurrence), "beyond the River" (subsequent) |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "beyond the River" (LOGOS), used consistently in vv. 2, 3, 14, 15. The Hebrew be'ever hannahar is a fixed geographical expression. "Beyond" is more concise and natural than "on the other side of." PNEUMA's specification of "Euphrates" at first mention is interpretive — the Hebrew says simply "the River," and the identification with the Euphrates, while correct, should remain in the reader's inference rather than being inserted into the text. Two-to-one support for "beyond the River" (LOGOS and PNEUMA's subsequent usage).

---

### Verse 2 — "Your fathers dwelt" (subject-first) vs. "On the other side... your fathers dwelt" (prepositional phrase first)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "On the other side of the River your fathers dwelt from of old" |
| LOGOS | "Your fathers dwelt beyond the River from of old" |
| PNEUMA | "Long ago your ancestors... lived beyond the Euphrates" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's word order. The Hebrew be'ever hannahar yashvu avoteikhem me'olam places the prepositional phrase first, but LOGOS's rendering reads more naturally in English while preserving all elements. EMET's Hebrew-order rendering is valid but slightly awkward. PNEUMA's "long ago" for me'olam and "ancestors" for avot depart from the standard SOPHIA rendering conventions (see below).

---

### Verse 2 — "from of old" vs. "long ago"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from of old" |
| LOGOS | "from of old" |
| PNEUMA | "long ago" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "from of old" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew me'olam conveys temporal remoteness with archaic gravity. "From of old" preserves this register. "Long ago" (PNEUMA) is accurate but flattens the elevated tone. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 3 — "led him" vs. "caused him to walk"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I caused him to walk through all the land of Canaan" |
| LOGOS | "I led him through all the land of Canaan" |
| PNEUMA | "led him through the whole land of Canaan" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "led him" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew Hiphil va'olekh (from halakh) means "I caused to go/walk." While EMET's causative "caused him to walk" is hyper-literal, "led" captures the Hiphil sense naturally — YHWH directed Abraham's journey. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 3 — "seed" vs. "descendants"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I multiplied his seed" |
| LOGOS | "I multiplied his seed" |
| PNEUMA | "I made his descendants many" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "his seed" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew zar'o ("his seed") is a key covenantal term throughout the patriarchal narratives. "Seed" preserves the agricultural metaphor and the intertextual resonance with Genesis 12-22. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 4 — "but Jacob" vs. "and Jacob"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and Yaakov and his sons went down [to] Mitsrayim" |
| LOGOS | "but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt" |
| PNEUMA | "but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "but" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew waw here carries a contrastive force — Esau received Seir and stayed, *but* Jacob went down to Egypt. The contrast between the two brothers' destinies is central to the narrative logic. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 5 — "struck" vs. "plagued"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I struck Egypt" |
| LOGOS | "I plagued Egypt" |
| PNEUMA | "I struck Egypt with the plagues I inflicted on them" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "struck" (EMET). The Hebrew va'aggof is from nagaf, "to strike/smite." While LOGOS's "plagued" and PNEUMA's expansion connect to the Exodus plague narrative, the Hebrew verb itself means "to strike," not specifically "to plague." "Struck" is the more faithful rendering. PNEUMA's expansion adds words not in the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 5 — "according to what I did" vs. "according to that which I did"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "according to that which I did in its midst" |
| LOGOS | "according to what I did in its midst" |
| PNEUMA | "with the plagues I inflicted on them" (restructured) |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "according to what I did in its midst." "What" is more natural than "that which" in modern English while preserving the Hebrew ka'asher asiti beqirbo. PNEUMA's restructuring departs from the Hebrew syntax.

---

### Verse 6 — "Egyptians" vs. "Egypt" (pursuing)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Mitsrayim pursued after your fathers" |
| LOGOS | "And the Egyptians pursued your fathers" |
| PNEUMA | "The Egyptians pursued your fathers" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the Egyptians" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew Mitsrayim can denote both the country and its people; in context, pursuers are people, not a land. "The Egyptians" clarifies this. Two-to-one agreement. SOPHIA also drops EMET's redundant "after" — the Hebrew vayyirdefu acharei has the preposition, but "pursued your fathers" in English already implies direction.

---

### Verse 7 — "that which I did" vs. "what I did"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "that which I did in Egypt" |
| LOGOS | "what I did in Egypt" |
| PNEUMA | "what I did to Egypt" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "what I did in Egypt." Same rationale as v. 5 — "what" is more natural than "that which." The preposition is "in" (be-Mitsrayim), not "to" (PNEUMA).

---

### Verse 8 — "who were dwelling" vs. "who dwelt" vs. "who lived"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the Amorites who were dwelling on the other side of the Jordan" |
| LOGOS | "the Amorites who dwelt on the other side of the Jordan" |
| PNEUMA | "the Amorites who lived east of the Jordan" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "who dwelt." The Hebrew participle hayyoshev can be rendered as either progressive ("were dwelling") or simple past ("dwelt"). "Dwelt" is more concise. PNEUMA's "lived east of" replaces the Hebrew idiom with compass interpretation; SOPHIA retains "on the other side of the Jordan."

---

### Verse 9 — "And" vs. "Then" (opening conjunction)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Balak son of Tsippor... arose and fought" |
| LOGOS | "Then Balak the son of Zippor... arose and fought" |
| PNEUMA | "Then Balak son of Zippor... set himself against Israel" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Then" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The wayyiqtol vayyaqom introduces a new episode in the historical sequence. "Then" marks the narrative transition more clearly than "and." Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 9 — "summoned" vs. "called for" vs. "sent for"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he sent and called for Balaam" |
| LOGOS | "he sent and summoned Balaam" |
| PNEUMA | "He sent for Balaam" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "he sent and summoned Balaam." The Hebrew vayyishlach vayyiqra preserves two verbs — "sent" and "called/summoned." EMET and LOGOS both preserve the double verb; PNEUMA collapses them into one. "Summoned" is preferred over "called for" per the v. 1 resolution.

---

### Verse 10 — "he surely blessed you" vs. "he blessed you repeatedly" vs. "he ended up blessing you again and again"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he surely blessed you" |
| LOGOS | "he blessed you repeatedly" |
| PNEUMA | "he ended up blessing you again and again" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "he surely blessed you" (EMET). The Hebrew barokh berakh is an infinitive absolute construction — the standard Hebrew emphatic doubling. Per the project rule on infinitive absolute with doubling, this must be rendered with the emphatic "surely" pattern. LOGOS's "repeatedly" and PNEUMA's "again and again" interpret the construction as iterative (referring to Balaam's multiple blessings in Numbers 23-24), which is contextually reasonable but does not reflect the grammar. The infinitive absolute intensifies the certainty and completeness of the blessing, not necessarily its repetition.

---

### Verse 10 — "But I was not willing" vs. "And I was not willing"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And I was not willing to listen to Balaam" |
| LOGOS | "But I was not willing to listen to Balaam" |
| PNEUMA | "but I would not listen to Balaam" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "But" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew waw here is adversative — Balak summoned Balaam to curse, *but* YHWH refused to listen. The contrast is essential to the narrative logic. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 11 — "lords" vs. "citizens"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the lords of Jericho" |
| LOGOS | "the lords of Jericho" |
| PNEUMA | "the citizens of Jericho" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the lords of Jericho" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew ba'alei yericho means "the masters/lords of Jericho" — ba'al denotes ownership and authority. "Citizens" (PNEUMA) flattens the social hierarchy implied by the term. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 12 — "the two kings" vs. "two kings"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "two kings of the Amorites" (no article) |
| LOGOS | "the two kings of the Amorites" (with article) |
| PNEUMA | "the two Amorite kings" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the two kings of the Amorites" (LOGOS). The Hebrew shenei malkhei ha'emori includes the definite article on "Amorite," and the numeral with a known referent (Sihon and Og) warrants the English definite article "the." EMET's omission of the article is less precise.

---

### Verse 13 — "toil" vs. "labor"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a land for which you did not toil" |
| LOGOS | "a land for which you did not labor" |
| PNEUMA | "a land you had not labored on" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "labor" (LOGOS). The Hebrew yaga'ta means "you labored/toiled." Both "toil" and "labor" are valid; "labor" is slightly more current. LOGOS's phrasing is also syntactically cleaner. PNEUMA's "labored on" adds a preposition not in the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 14 — "in completeness and in truth" vs. "in wholeness and in faithfulness" vs. "with complete integrity and faithfulness"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in completeness and in truth" |
| LOGOS | "in wholeness and in faithfulness" |
| PNEUMA | "with complete integrity and faithfulness" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "in wholeness and in faithfulness" (LOGOS). The Hebrew betamim uve'emet pairs two nouns with the preposition be-. Tamim means "complete, whole, without defect" — "wholeness" captures the covenantal sense of undivided devotion better than "completeness" (which can sound quantitative). 'Emet means "truth, faithfulness, reliability" — "faithfulness" captures the relational dimension better than the abstract "truth." LOGOS preserves the Hebrew prepositional structure ("in... and in...") that PNEUMA restructures. The LXX Vaticanus reads "in uprightness and in truth," supporting the dual-prepositional structure.

---

### Verse 14 — "Now therefore" vs. "And now" vs. "So now"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And now" |
| LOGOS | "Now therefore" |
| PNEUMA | "So now" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Now therefore" (LOGOS). The Hebrew ve'attah introduces the hortatory conclusion drawn from the historical recital — because YHWH has done all this, *therefore* fear and serve him. "Now therefore" captures the consequential force better than the plain "and now" (EMET) or the informal "so now" (PNEUMA).

---

### Verse 15 — "But as for me" vs. "And as for me"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And as for me and my house" |
| LOGOS | "But as for me and my house" |
| PNEUMA | "As for me and my household" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "But as for me and my house" (LOGOS). The Hebrew ve'anokhi uveiti opens with the emphatic independent pronoun, creating a dramatic contrast with whatever the people choose. "But" captures this adversative-contrastive force — Joshua's declaration stands against all other options. "House" is preferred over "household" (PNEUMA) as the literal rendering of bayit. Two-to-one support for the contrastive particle (LOGOS's "but," PNEUMA's implied contrast through sentence break).

---

### Verse 16 — "forsake" vs. "abandon"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Far be it from us to forsake YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "Far be it from us to forsake YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "Far be it from us to abandon YHWH" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "forsake" (EMET/LOGOS). Consistent with Joshua 1:5 resolution — "forsake" carries stronger covenantal weight than "abandon." Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 17 — "house of slaves" vs. "house of bondage" vs. "house of slavery"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from the house of slaves" |
| LOGOS | "from the house of bondage" |
| PNEUMA | "from the house of slavery" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "house of bondage" (LOGOS). The Hebrew beit avadim is the standard Exodus formula (Exodus 13:3, 14; 20:2; Deuteronomy 5:6). "House of bondage" is the classic English rendering that preserves the formulaic quality and links directly to the Decalogue prologue. EMET's "house of slaves" is technically literal (avadim = slaves/servants) but loses the fixed-phrase resonance. PNEUMA's "house of slavery" is close but less established.

---

### Verse 17 — "kept us" vs. "preserved us" vs. "guarded us"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he kept us in all the way which we walked" |
| LOGOS | "preserved us in all the way that we went" |
| PNEUMA | "guarded us along the entire road we traveled" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "preserved us" (LOGOS). The Hebrew vayyishmerenu (from shamar, "to keep, guard, preserve") allows several renderings. "Preserved" conveys both protection and maintenance through danger, which fits the wilderness journey context. "Kept" (EMET) is more ambiguous in English. "Guarded" (PNEUMA) is valid but more militaristic. SOPHIA also follows LOGOS's "in all the way that we went" — preserving the Hebrew preposition and relative clause structure.

---

### Verse 17 — "signs" vs. "signs"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "these great signs" |
| LOGOS | "these great signs" |
| PNEUMA | "these great signs" |

**Resolution:** Full agreement. SOPHIA retains "these great signs."

---

### Verse 18 — "Also we" vs. "We also" vs. "We too"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Also we will serve YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "We also will serve YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "We too will serve YHWH" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "We also will serve YHWH." The Hebrew gam anachnu places the emphatic particle gam ("also, even") before the pronoun. "We also" places the emphasis naturally in English. EMET's "Also we" follows Hebrew word order but is awkward. PNEUMA's "We too" is acceptable but slightly less formal.

---

### Verse 19 — "transgressions" vs. "rebellion"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "your transgressions and your sins" |
| LOGOS | "your transgressions and your sins" |
| PNEUMA | "your rebellion and your sins" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "transgressions" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew pesha' means "transgression, rebellion, willful violation." While "rebellion" (PNEUMA) captures the willful aspect, "transgressions" is broader and more standard for pesha' in the biblical register. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 20 — "do evil to you" vs. "do you harm" vs. "bring disaster on you"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he will turn and do evil to you" |
| LOGOS | "he will turn and do you harm" |
| PNEUMA | "he will turn against you. He will bring disaster on you" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "he will turn and do you harm." The Hebrew veherai' lakhem uses the Hiphil of ra'a — "to do evil/harm to." "Do you harm" (LOGOS) is natural English that preserves the single Hebrew clause structure. EMET's "do evil to you" is literal but "do evil" in English implies moral wrongdoing by YHWH, which is misleading; the Hiphil here means to inflict harm/calamity. PNEUMA splits into two sentences and adds "against you," expanding beyond the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 20 — "after that he has done good" vs. "after he has done good"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "after that he has done good to you" |
| LOGOS | "after he has done good to you" |
| PNEUMA | "after all the good he has done for you" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "after he has done good to you." The demonstrative "that" in EMET is unnecessary in English. PNEUMA's "all the good" adds a word not in the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 21 — "No, for YHWH we will serve" vs. "No, but YHWH we will serve"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "No, for YHWH we will serve" |
| LOGOS | "No, but YHWH we will serve" |
| PNEUMA | "No! We will serve YHWH" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "No, but YHWH we will serve." The Hebrew lo ki et-YHWH na'avod uses ki as an adversative particle following the negative — "No, but rather..." LOGOS's "but" captures this. EMET's "for" misreads the ki as causal. PNEUMA drops the adversative force and normalizes the word order, losing the emphatic fronting of YHWH.

---

### Verse 22 — "Witnesses [are] you" vs. "You are witnesses"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Witnesses [are] you against yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "You are witnesses against yourselves" |
| PNEUMA | "You are witnesses against yourselves" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "You are witnesses against yourselves." Normal English word order. EMET's Hebrew-fronted word order is awkward. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 25 — "cut a covenant" vs. "made a covenant"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Joshua cut a covenant for the people" |
| LOGOS | "Joshua made a covenant with the people" |
| PNEUMA | "Joshua made a covenant with the people" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "made a covenant." The Hebrew vayyikhrot berit uses the verb karath ("to cut"), and "cut a covenant" is the literal idiom. While EMET preserves the Hebrew metaphor (referring to the cutting of sacrificial animals in covenant ceremony), "made a covenant" is the conventional English rendering and immediately comprehensible. Two-to-one agreement. The Hebrew idiom is noted for scholarly interest.

---

### Verse 25 — "judgment" vs. "ordinance"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a statute and a judgment" |
| LOGOS | "a statute and an ordinance" |
| PNEUMA | "a statute and an ordinance" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "a statute and an ordinance." The Hebrew choq umishpat pairs two legal terms. Mishpat can mean "judgment" or "ordinance/ruling." In this legal-codification context, "ordinance" (a binding legal decree) is more precise than the broader "judgment." Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 26 — "scroll" vs. "book" vs. "Book of the Instruction"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the scroll of the Torah of God" |
| LOGOS | "the book of the Torah of God" |
| PNEUMA | "the Book of the Instruction of God" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the scroll of the Torah of God" — combining EMET's "scroll" with the standard "Torah." Consistent with Joshua 1:8 resolution, where "scroll" was preferred for sefer as historically accurate for Joshua's period. "Torah" is retained as the standard untranslated term (EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "Instruction," which, while a valid translation of torah, loses the technical designation.

---

### Verse 26 — "raised it up" vs. "set it up"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "raised it up there" |
| LOGOS | "set it up there" |
| PNEUMA | "set it up there" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "set it up there." The Hebrew vayyeqimeha (from qum, Hiphil) means "to cause to stand, to erect, to set up." "Set it up" is the natural English for erecting a standing stone. Two-to-one agreement.

---

### Verse 27 — "sayings" vs. "words"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all the sayings of YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "all the words of YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "all the words that YHWH spoke" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "all the words of YHWH." The Hebrew imrei YHWH uses the plural of emer/imrah, which can mean "words" or "sayings." "Words" is more standard and matches the usual rendering of divine speech. Two-to-one preference for "words."

---

### Verse 27 — "deny your God" vs. "deal falsely with your God"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "lest you deny your God" |
| LOGOS | "lest you deal falsely with your God" |
| PNEUMA | "so that you do not deny your God" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: "lest you deny your God." The Hebrew pen tekachashun be'eloheikhem uses the verb kachash, which means "to deny, to be false, to disown." "Deny" is the primary meaning and more direct than LOGOS's circumlocution "deal falsely with." Two-to-one agreement (EMET and PNEUMA both use "deny"). SOPHIA retains the formal "lest" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "so that you do not."

---

### Verse 28 — "each to his inheritance" vs. "each man to his inheritance" vs. "each to their own inheritance"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "each to his inheritance" |
| LOGOS | "each man to his inheritance" |
| PNEUMA | "each to their own inheritance" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "each man to his inheritance." The Hebrew ish lenachalato specifies ish ("man/each one") with the singular possessive. "Each man" preserves the Hebrew ish. PNEUMA's plural "their" departs from the singular. EMET drops ish from the English.

---

### Verse 29 — "And it was after these things, and Joshua... died" vs. "And it was after these things that Joshua... died"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And it was after these things, and Joshua son of Nun, servant of YHWH, died" |
| LOGOS | "And it was after these things that Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died" |
| PNEUMA | "After these things, Joshua son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The wayehi acharei hadevarim ha'elleh formula is followed by a subordinated clause. LOGOS's "that" correctly captures the syntax (as resolved in Joshua 1:1). EMET's second "and" is hyper-literal. PNEUMA drops the wayehi entirely.

---

### Verse 29 — Age phrasing: "a son of one hundred and ten years" vs. "being one hundred and ten years old"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[being] a son of one hundred and ten years" |
| LOGOS | "being one hundred and ten years old" |
| PNEUMA | "He was one hundred and ten years old" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "being one hundred and ten years old." The Hebrew ben me'ah va'eser shanim literally means "a son of one hundred and ten years" — the standard Hebrew age formula. LOGOS preserves this as a participial phrase modifying Joshua, which is elegant and accurate. EMET's ultra-literal "a son of" is the Hebrew idiom but opaque in English. PNEUMA creates a new sentence, breaking the Hebrew's single-clause structure.

---

### Verse 30 — "border" vs. "territory" vs. "borders"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in the border of his inheritance" |
| LOGOS | "in the territory of his inheritance" |
| PNEUMA | "within the borders of his inheritance" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "in the territory of his inheritance." The Hebrew bigvul nachalato uses gevul, which can mean "border" or "territory." In context, Joshua is buried *within* his allotted territory, not on its boundary line. "Territory" (LOGOS) is the clearest rendering. Consistent with Joshua 1:4 resolution.

---

### Verse 30 — "on the north of" vs. "on the north side of" vs. "north of"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "on the north of Mount Gaash" |
| LOGOS | "on the north side of Mount Gaash" |
| PNEUMA | "north of Mount Gaash" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET: "on the north of Mount Gaash." The Hebrew mitzsefon lehar ga'ash means "from the north of Mount Gaash." EMET's concise rendering preserves the preposition. LOGOS's "side" adds a word. PNEUMA's bare "north of" drops the Hebrew preposition min.

---

### Verse 31 — "prolonged days" vs. "prolonged their days" vs. "outlived"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the elders who prolonged days after Joshua" |
| LOGOS | "the elders who prolonged their days after Joshua" |
| PNEUMA | "the elders who outlived Joshua" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "the elders who prolonged their days after Joshua." The Hebrew he'erikhu yamim acharei Yehoshua literally means "who lengthened days after Joshua." LOGOS preserves the literal sense with the possessive "their." PNEUMA's "outlived" is dynamic and loses the Hebrew idiom. EMET drops the possessive.

---

### Verse 31 — "knew" vs. "had known" vs. "had personally experienced"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who knew all the work of YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "who had known all the work of YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "who had personally experienced everything YHWH had done" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "who had known all the work of YHWH." The pluperfect "had known" correctly places the knowing prior to the narrative present. EMET's simple past is adequate but less precise temporally. PNEUMA's "personally experienced" and "everything" expand beyond the Hebrew yad'u et-kol-ma'aseh.

---

### Verse 32 — "Sons of Israel" — agreement

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the sons of Israel" (lowercase "sons") |
| LOGOS | "the Sons of Israel" (capitalized) |
| PNEUMA | "the Sons of Israel" (capitalized) |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the Sons of Israel" with capitalized "Sons," per project convention. Two-to-one agreement on capitalization.

---

### Verse 32 — "acquired" vs. "bought"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "which Jacob acquired from the sons of Hamor" |
| LOGOS | "that Jacob had acquired from the sons of Hamor" |
| PNEUMA | "that Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "that Jacob had acquired." The Hebrew qanah means "to acquire, buy, purchase." "Acquired" is slightly broader and more formal than "bought," and two of three translators use it. The pluperfect "had acquired" (LOGOS) correctly marks the action as prior to the burial.

---

### Verse 32 — "[an inheritance] for the sons of Joseph" vs. "an inheritance for the sons of Joseph" vs. "the inheritance of the descendants of Joseph"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "they became [an inheritance] for the sons of Yosef" |
| LOGOS | "they became an inheritance for the sons of Joseph" |
| PNEUMA | "It became the inheritance of the descendants of Joseph" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "they became an inheritance for the sons of Joseph." LOGOS supplies "an inheritance" clearly (the Hebrew linachalat benei Yosef means "for an inheritance for the sons of Joseph"). PNEUMA's "descendants" departs from the literal "sons" (benei).

---

### Verse 33 — "Gibeah" vs. "Hill of Phinehas" vs. "the hill of Phinehas"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in Giv'at Phinehas his son" |
| LOGOS | "in the hill of Phinehas his son" |
| PNEUMA | "at Gibeah, the town of his son Phinehas" |

**Resolution:** SOPHIA renders "in the Hill of Phinehas his son." The Hebrew begiv'at Pinechas beno is ambiguous — giv'ah means "hill" and Giv'at Pinechas may be either a place name ("Gibeah of Phinehas") or a descriptive phrase ("the hill of Phinehas"). SOPHIA capitalizes "Hill" to signal that this functions as a proper place designation while preserving the literal Hebrew meaning. PNEUMA's addition of "the town of" is not in the Hebrew. The possessive order "Phinehas his son" preserves the Hebrew construction.
